title: What is a method
instruction:
A method is just a function with one extra thing: a receiver.

The receiver goes between `func` and the method name:

```
func (p Person) Greet() {
    fmt.Println("Hi, I'm", p.Name)
}
```

That `(p Person)` part says: this function belongs to the `Person` type. Call it with `p.Greet()` — not `Greet(p)`.

Run this. Then change the greeting to include the person's age too.
hint: fmt.Printf("Hi, I'm %s and I'm %d years old\n", p.Name, p.Age)

codeChecks.message: Include p.Age in the greeting.

successMessage: p.Greet() — clean and readable. The method knows it belongs to Person. That is the whole idea.

title: Define a method
instruction:
Write your own method.

Add a `Describe()` method to `Car` that prints: `"2020 Toyota Camry"`

The format is: `Year Make Model`.

Then call it.
hint: func (c Car) Describe() { fmt.Println(c.Year, c.Make, c.Model) }

codeChecks.message: Define a Describe() method on Car: func (c Car) Describe() { ... }
Call c.Describe() in main.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Your method, your type, your call. The Car now knows how to describe itself.

title: Value receiver — reads but does not change
instruction:
A value receiver receives a copy of the struct. Changes inside the method do not affect the original.

This is fine when you are only reading — like printing, calculating, comparing.

Run this. Notice that calling `tryRename` does not change `p.Name` — because it got a copy.
successMessage: Jordan inside, Alex outside. The method changed its copy, not the original. Value receivers are safe for read-only operations — nothing unexpected can happen.

title: Pointer receiver — actually changes the struct
instruction:
To modify the original struct from a method, use a pointer receiver: `(p *Person)`.

Now the method receives the address — not a copy — and changes stick.

Add a `Birthday()` method with a pointer receiver that increments `p.Age`. Call it and print the age before and after.

codeChecks.message: Define Birthday() with a pointer receiver: func (p *Person) Birthday() { p.Age++ }
Call p.Birthday() and print p.Age after.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: 29 before, 30 after. The pointer receiver method reached into the original struct and changed it. Happy birthday, Sam.

title: Value vs pointer receiver — side by side
instruction:
Rule of thumb:
- **Reading only?** Use a value receiver
- **Mutating the struct?** Use a pointer receiver
- **Large struct?** Use a pointer receiver to avoid copying

Run this. Both methods live on `Counter`. `Show()` reads, `Increment()` mutates.
successMessage: Show uses a value receiver — it only reads. Increment uses a pointer receiver — it mutates. Mix both on the same type freely, just follow the rule.

title: Methods with parameters
instruction:
Methods can accept parameters just like regular functions.

`func (r Rectangle) Scale(factor float64) Rectangle`

This takes a `factor`, scales the rectangle, and returns a new one.

Add a call that scales the rectangle by 2 and prints the new dimensions.

codeChecks.message: Call r.Scale(2) to scale the rectangle.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 5×3 scaled by 2 becomes 10×6. Methods with parameters are just functions — same syntax, same rules, just attached to a type.

title: Methods with return values
instruction:
Methods return values the same way functions do.

Add two methods to `Rectangle`:
- `Area() float64` — returns width × height
- `Perimeter() float64` — returns 2 × (width + height)

Call both and print the results.
hint: func (r Rectangle) Area() float64 { return r.Width * r.Height }

codeChecks.message: Add Area() float64 method to Rectangle.
Add Perimeter() float64 method to Rectangle.
Replace the TODO comments with your methods.

successMessage: 6×4 = area 24, perimeter 20. The Rectangle type now knows how to calculate its own properties. That is object-oriented thinking in Go.

title: Multiple methods on one type
instruction:
A type can have as many methods as you need. They all live separately in code but belong to the same type.

Run this. `Rectangle` now has `Area()`, `Perimeter()`, and `IsSquare()`. Then add a `Describe()` method that prints all three in one line.

Format: `"6 x 4 | Area: 24.00 | Perimeter: 20.00 | Square: false"`
hint: func (r Rectangle) Describe() { fmt.Printf("%.0f x %.0f | Area: %.2f | Perimeter: %.2f | Square: %v\n", r.Width, r.Height, r.Area(), r.Perimeter(), r.IsSquare()) }

codeChecks.message: Add a Describe() method to Rectangle.
Call r.Area() inside Describe.
Call r.Perimeter() inside Describe.
Call r.IsSquare() inside Describe.
Replace the TODO comment with your method.

successMessage: One Describe() calls three other methods. Methods calling methods — this is how real programs are structured.

title: Return self for sequential calls
instruction:
Go does not have built-in method chaining. But if your method returns a pointer to the same type, you can call multiple methods in sequence.

`b.SetTitle("Go").SetPrice(29.99).Print()`

Run this and see how each setter returns `*Book` to allow the next call.
successMessage: Each setter returns the pointer to self, enabling the next call. You see this pattern in query builders, HTTP clients, and configuration APIs all over Go.

title: Methods on non-struct types
instruction:
You can add methods to any named type — not just structs.

`type Celsius float64` is a named type based on `float64`. You can attach methods to it.

Add a `ToKelvin()` method to `Celsius` that returns `float64(c + 273.15)`. Call it and print the result.
hint: func (c Celsius) ToKelvin() float64 { return float64(c + 273.15) }

codeChecks.message: Add a ToKelvin() float64 method to Celsius.
Add 273.15 to convert Celsius to Kelvin.
Call temp.ToKelvin() and print the result.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Methods on a float64-based type. You just made numbers smarter. This pattern is used for units, currencies, IDs — any type where the underlying value has domain-specific behaviour.

title: String() — fmt prints it automatically
instruction:
If you add a `String() string` method to your type, `fmt.Println` will call it automatically.

This is Go's way of letting your types control how they are printed — no special interface keyword needed yet, just the right method signature.

Add a `String() string` method to `Temperature` that returns `"23.50°C"`. Then just call `fmt.Println(t)` and see it use your method.
hint: func (t Temperature) String() string { return fmt.Sprintf("%.2f°C", t.Value) }

codeChecks.message: Add String() string method to Temperature.
Use fmt.Sprintf to format the string inside String().
Replace the TODO comment with your method.

successMessage: fmt.Println called your String() method without you asking it to. This is Go's interface system at work — and you just used it without knowing. Next chapter: Interfaces.

title: Fix the broken method
instruction:
The `Restock` method below is supposed to add items to a product's stock. But it does not work — the stock stays at 10 after the call.

Find the bug and fix it.
hint: The method uses a value receiver (p Product). Change it to a pointer receiver (p *Product) so it modifies the original.

codeChecks.message: Change the receiver to *Product: func (p *Product) Restock(amount int)

successMessage: One character change — (p Product) to (p *Product) — and it works. The most common method bug in Go. You will never forget this.

title: Build a shape calculator
instruction:
No starter code.

Build two types with their own methods:

**Circle** (radius float64)
- `Area() float64` → π × r²  (use 3.14159 for π)
- `Perimeter() float64` → 2 × π × r
- `Describe()` → prints `"Circle | r=5.00 | Area: 78.54 | Perimeter: 31.42"`

**Rectangle** (Width, Height float64)
- `Area() float64` → w × h
- `Perimeter() float64` → 2 × (w + h)
- `Describe()` → prints `"Rectangle | 8.00 x 3.00 | Area: 24.00 | Perimeter: 22.00"`

Create one of each and call Describe on both.
hint: const pi = 3.14159  func (c Circle) Area() float64 { return pi * c.Radius * c.Radius }

codeChecks.message: Define a Circle struct.
Define a Rectangle struct.
Add an Area() method to Circle.
Add a Perimeter() method to Circle.
Add a Describe() method to Circle.
Add an Area() method to Rectangle.
Add a Perimeter() method to Rectangle.
Add a Describe() method to Rectangle.

successMessage: Two types. Six methods. One clean program. You just built a fully object-oriented shape calculator in Go — without a single class keyword. That is the Go way.
